title: Built this because every trading “AI tool” felt like a black box
author: Terrible_Emphasis473
selftext:
https://preview.redd.it/0xyptww4zapg1.png?width=2402&format=png&auto=webp&s=2f8bf768740a8d235e0d0d6592ed2ee9e03c4c24

Over the last year I kept trying different “AI trading” and market signal tools and ran into the same problem over and over again: you can’t see what’s actually happening under the hood.

Everything feels like magic until it stops working.

So I decided to build my own stack where every part of the pipeline is explicit — data ingest, sentiment analysis, ML inference, alerts, and backtesting — instead of one giant opaque model.

A few things I wanted:

• **Multiple data sources** instead of one provider (market feeds, macro data, news, social sentiment)  
• **Real-time ingestion + streaming** so signals don’t lag  
• **Worker architecture** where each task (sentiment, ingest, alerts, analytics) runs independently  
• **Backtesting built into the pipeline** instead of as an afterthought  
• **A simple frontend** to actually visualize what the system is doing

Stack ended up looking roughly like this:

Frontend  
React / Next.js

API layer  
Node + Express / tRPC

Workers  
Market ingest  
News + sentiment processing  
ML inference  
Alerts / notifications  
Backtesting & analytics

Infrastructure  
Postgres  
Redis  
Kafka / PubSub  
Object storage

External data  
Market feeds  
Macro data  
Financial news  
Social sentiment APIs  
LLM processing

The goal wasn’t to build a “magic trading bot.”  
It was to build something where **every signal has a traceable pipeline**.

Right now it’s basically a radar for market signals rather than an auto-trader.

Curious if anyone else here has tried building something similar — or if people are mostly still relying on closed platforms.

replies.author: Terrible_Emphasis473
replies.body:
Not really. Those lean more toward automated trading or signal selling.

This is more like an **infrastructure layer** for aggregating signals and analyzing them — market data, news sentiment, macro inputs, etc — and then being able to **trace exactly how a signal was produced**.

Less “AI trader,” more **observable signal pipeline**.

replies.author: SergeiStorm
replies.body:
Sounds interesting - but who is the actual user?
If it’s not automated trading and not signal selling, who benefits from the infrastructure layer?

replies.author: Terrible_Emphasis473
replies.body:
Good question. The users are mostly people who already build strategies or models but struggle with the *data plumbing*.

Quants, small funds, independent researchers, even devs experimenting with trading ideas.

Most of them already have models — the pain point is stitching together:

market feeds  
news sentiment  
macro data  
social signals  
backtesting  
real-time alerts

and being able to see **why a signal happened**, not just that it happened.

So the idea is less “press button → trade” and more **an observable pipeline you can build strategies on top of.**

Kind of like the difference between a trading bot and **building your own Bloomberg-style signal infrastructure.**

replies.author: SergeiStorm
replies.body: Cool architecture, yet most quant failures come from mistaking noise for signal, not from missing data pipelines. My concern would be that more data sources sometimes just amplify noise rather than improve signals. Anyway, good luck with your project.

replies.author: Terrible_Emphasis473
replies.body:
That’s a really good point. The “more data = better signals” assumption is definitely dangerous.

What I’m trying to experiment with is treating the extra sources more like *context filters* rather than direct signals — e.g. using them to confirm/deny moves rather than generate trades themselves.

Still early though, so there’s a decent chance I’m just building a very elaborate noise machine

comments.author: StackMotive
comments.body:
*The transparency problem is real and I ran into exactly the same thing. The "magic signal" tools give you an output with no lineage, you don't know if the bullish score is driven by price momentum, sentiment, or someone's proprietary weighting that changes without notice.*

*Your worker architecture approach is the right call for auditability. Separating ingest, inference, and alerts into independent pipelines means when something fires wrong you can actually trace it.*

*Curious how you're handling the macro layer, specifically Fed regime signals and whether they gate your ML inference or run in parallel. That's the part I found hardest to get right. A signal that's valid in risk-on becomes noise in risk-off and most retail tools don't make that distinction.*

replies.author: Terrible_Emphasis473
replies.body:
Appreciate that — the lineage problem is exactly what pushed me to build it this way. A lot of the “AI signal” tools feel like black boxes where you get a score but no explanation of why it fired.

For macro right now I’m not doing hard gating yet. I’m treating regime signals more like a weighting layer that adjusts confidence rather than turning models on/off. Still experimenting though — I’ve noticed the same issue where signals that look great in risk-on completely fall apart once volatility regimes shift.

Curious if you ended up solving that with explicit regime classification or more of a volatility / liquidity proxy?

replies.author: StackMotive
replies.body:
*Both actually, layered. Explicit regime classification first, Fed stance, yield curve shape, VIX regime, M2 trend to set the macro context. Then volatility/liquidity proxies as the real-time signal that something is shifting before the classification catches up.*

*The classification tells you what game you're playing. The volatility proxy tells you the rules are changing before the scoreboard reflects it.*

*The weighting approach you're using is probably more robust than hard gating for a bot, hard gating creates cliff edges where a signal goes from full weight to zero and you get weird behaviour at the boundary. Confidence weighting degrades more gracefully.*

*I ended up building this into a platform rather than a standalone bot (currently in Beta testing) The regime layer sits above the signal confluence engine and adjusts how individual signals are weighted depending on macro context. Happy to show you how it handles it if you're curious.*

comments.author: dragon_dudee
comments.body:
I am doing something similar in terms of architecture at tradehorde.ai
In addition all of the analysis is shown and if a directional signal is produced it is tracked all the way to outcomes.

There are many market analysis and signal producing apps, how many score their own signals in public? That’s the difference I am trying to create.

replies.author: Terrible_Emphasis473
replies.body:
That’s interesting — the part I like there is tracking signals all the way to outcomes. That’s something a lot of tools avoid because it exposes whether the signals actually work or not.

The transparency piece is what I’m trying to experiment with too. Most platforms output a “score” but you can’t really see the pipeline that produced it.

Curious — are you mostly generating signals from technical data, sentiment sources, or a mix?

replies.author: dragon_dudee
replies.body: A lot of what you do :) multiple sources like you do and very similar frontend and backend stack as well. I serve a buffet to the models and then let them decide what sources they want to choose.

replies.author: Terrible_Emphasis473
replies.body:
Interesting approach. Letting the models choose from a “buffet” of sources makes sense, especially as long as you can still trace which inputs actually drove the signal.

One thing I’m experimenting with is keeping the pipelines a bit more explicit so I can see when a specific data source starts degrading. Otherwise it’s hard to tell if performance drift is coming from the model or the inputs.

Curious how you’re handling attribution — do you track which sources the model actually relied on for each signal?

replies.author: dragon_dudee
replies.body: Yes. I do. There are some takeaways already, especially tying tool usage to win rate and seeing which ones are more effective and which ones are worthless. So kind of nudging in the prompt to lean into some of this tools. Models can still choose to ignore the nudge.

comments.author: GarbageTimePro
comments.body: This entire thread is just a bunch of ai agents talking to each other.

comments.author: CapMonster1
comments.body: Transparency beats magic alpha every time, especially when markets change regimes. The worker separation and built-in backtesting is a big deal; most tools bolt that on later and it shows. One thing to watch if you’re ingesting multiple external feeds at scale is API rate limits and anti-bot layers a lot of financial/news endpoints quietly enforce CAPTCHA or challenge flows once volume increases. Some teams isolate that layer behind a dedicated solver API (e.g., CapMonster Cloud) so ingestion workers don’t stall when a provider throws reCAPTCHA/Turnstile at them. If you ever want to stress-test that part of the pipeline, we’re happy to share a small test balance for Reddit users. Curious whether you’re planning to keep this as a signal radar or eventually let it execute trades automatically.

comments.author: melbkiwi
comments.body: This is the right direction. A lot of “AI trading” stuff falls apart because you can’t inspect the chain from input to signal to outcome, so when performance drifts you don’t know whether the model broke, the data changed, or the regime shifted. Transparency doesn’t guarantee edge, but it does at least make failure diagnosable.
